items.title: Millions start work too early. This drug helps them stay awake
items.summary: Millions of people start work before sunrise—but their brains aren’t ready for it. A new clinical trial has found that the wake-promoting drug solriamfetol can significantly boost alertness in early-morning shift workers struggling with shift work disorder. Participants who took the drug were able to stay awake and function better throughout full shifts, with improvements in productivity, safety, and daily performance.

items.title: Scientists found a baby dinosaur hidden in rock and it is surprisingly cute
items.summary: Scientists uncovered a rare baby dinosaur in South Korea and named it Doolysaurus after a famous cartoon character. Using cutting-edge CT scans, they discovered hidden bones—including a skull—inside rock much faster than traditional methods. The young dinosaur, possibly fluffy and lamb-like, even had stomach stones that reveal it ate a mix of plants and small animals. The discovery suggests many more dinosaurs may still be hidden in Korea’s rocks.

items.title: Scientists open 40-year-old salmon and find a surprising sign of ocean recovery
items.summary: Old canned salmon turned out to be a time capsule of ocean health. Researchers found that rising levels of tiny parasitic worms in some salmon species suggest stronger, more complete marine food webs. Because these parasites depend on multiple hosts—including marine mammals—their increase may reflect ecosystem recovery over decades. What looks unappetizing may actually be a sign of a healthier ocean.

items.title: NASA’s asteroid Bennu sample reveals a hidden chemical patchwork
items.summary: Scientists studying Bennu samples have discovered that its chemistry is far from uniform. Organic compounds and minerals cluster into three distinct types of regions, each shaped differently by past water activity. This uneven pattern shows that water altered the asteroid in a complex, localized way. The survival of delicate organic molecules adds an important clue to how life’s building blocks may persist in space.

items.title: Hardly anyone uses this surprisingly simple blood pressure fix
items.summary: Salt substitutes offer a simple, low-cost way to reduce sodium intake and improve blood pressure, yet very few Americans actually use them—even among those who need them most. A large national analysis spanning nearly two decades found that usage remains surprisingly low and has not improved over time. Researchers say this gap represents a major missed opportunity for preventing heart disease and stroke.

items.title: Scientists just found DNA “supergenes” that speed up evolution
items.summary: Hidden within fish DNA are powerful genetic twists that may explain one of nature’s biggest mysteries: how new species form so quickly. In Lake Malawi, hundreds of cichlid fish species evolved at lightning speed, and scientists now think “flipped” sections of DNA—called chromosomal inversions—are the secret. These inversions lock together useful gene combinations, creating “supergenes” that help fish rapidly adapt to different environments, from deep waters to sandy shores.

items.title: Coffee at night may increase risky behavior, especially in women
items.summary: Drinking coffee at night might come with an unexpected downside: increased impulsivity. In a new study, fruit flies given caffeine after dark behaved more recklessly, ignoring signals that normally make them stop. Daytime caffeine didn’t have the same effect, pointing to a strong link between timing and behavior. Even more striking, females were far more sensitive than males.

items.title: Scientists discover hidden “winds” inside cells that could explain cancer spread
items.summary: Cells aren’t as passive as scientists once thought—they actively create internal currents to move proteins quickly and efficiently. These “cellular winds” push materials to the front of the cell, enabling faster movement and repair. Discovered by chance and confirmed with advanced imaging, this system challenges decades of textbook biology. It may also reveal why some cancer cells spread so rapidly.

items.title: How squid survived Earth’s biggest extinction and took over the oceans
items.summary: Scientists have finally cracked a long-standing mystery about squid and cuttlefish evolution by analyzing newly sequenced genomes alongside global datasets. The research reveals that these bizarre, intelligent creatures likely originated deep in the ocean over 100 million years ago, surviving mass extinction events by retreating into oxygen-rich deep-sea refuges. For millions of years, their evolution barely changed—until a dramatic post-extinction boom sparked rapid diversification as they move

items.title: Scientists discover sleep switch that builds muscle, burns fat, and boosts brainpower
items.summary: Deep sleep does far more than rest the body — it activates a powerful brain-driven system that controls growth hormone, fueling muscle and bone strength, metabolism, and even mental performance. Scientists have now mapped the neural circuits behind this process, uncovering a delicate feedback loop in which sleep boosts growth hormone, and that same hormone helps regulate wakefulness.

items.title: Simple therapies beat drugs for knee arthritis pain relief
items.summary: A major analysis of nearly 10,000 patients shows that simple, non-drug treatments like knee braces, hydrotherapy, and exercise can significantly ease knee osteoarthritis symptoms. These approaches not only reduce pain and improve mobility, but also avoid the risks tied to common medications. The findings suggest that low-cost, accessible therapies could play a bigger role in how doctors treat arthritis in the future.

items.title: Webb telescope spots mysterious explosion that defies known physics
items.summary: Astronomers have spotted a bizarre cosmic explosion that refuses to play by the rules—and it’s leaving scientists scrambling for answers. GRB 250702B, detected by NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope and a global network of observatories, lasted an astonishing seven hours—far longer than typical gamma-ray bursts, which usually fade in under a minute.

ai_report.key_takeaway: Recent scientific discoveries range from advancements in sleep and muscle growth to insights into ancient marine life and the evolution of squid.
ai_report.summary: Today's science headlines highlight significant findings, including a drug that aids early shift workers, the discovery of a baby dinosaur, and insights into ocean health from old salmon. Other notable studies reveal the role of DNA supergenes in rapid evolution, the effects of nighttime caffeine on behavior, and the discovery of cellular mechanisms that may explain cancer spread. Additionally, researchers found that simple therapies can effectively relieve knee arthritis pain, while the Webb telescope observed a mysterious cosmic explosion.
key_themes: impact of drugs on shift work

notable_headlines.headline: Millions start work too early. This drug helps them stay awake
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: The drug solriamfetol significantly improves alertness for early-morning shift workers, enhancing productivity and safety.

notable_headlines.headline: Scientists found a baby dinosaur hidden in rock and it is surprisingly cute
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: The discovery of Doolysaurus suggests that many more dinosaur fossils may be hidden in South Korea.

notable_headlines.headline: Scientists open 40-year-old salmon and find a surprising sign of ocean recovery
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: The increase of parasitic worms in salmon indicates a healthier marine ecosystem, reflecting decades of recovery.

notable_headlines.headline: Scientists just found DNA “supergenes” that speed up evolution
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: The identification of supergenes in fish DNA sheds light on rapid species evolution in diverse environments.

notable_headlines.headline: Coffee at night may increase risky behavior, especially in women
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: The study highlights the potential behavioral risks associated with nighttime caffeine consumption, particularly in females.

notable_headlines.headline: Scientists discover hidden “winds” inside cells that could explain cancer spread
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: The discovery of cellular currents challenges traditional biology and may explain rapid cancer cell movement.

next_24_72_hours: Further analysis of the baby dinosaur's ecosystem may be conducted.

caveats: The long-term effects of solriamfetol are still unknown.
The implications of the baby dinosaur discovery require further research to understand its significance.
The relationship between caffeine consumption and behavior may vary among individuals.
